question.text: A put option on USDINR with strike Rs 84.00 has a premium of Rs 0.50 when USDINR spot is Rs 83.50. What is its intrinsic value?

answer.text: Rs 0.50 (K - S = 84.00 - 83.50 = 0.50, and the premium is also 0.50, so time value is zero)
Rs 0 (the put is out of the money)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Put intrinsic = max(K - S, 0) = max(84.00 - 83.50, 0) = Rs 0.50. Premium = Rs 0.50. So time value = 0.50 - 0.50 = Rs 0. This option is ITM with intrinsic equal to premium (likely near expiry). **The Trap:** The put IS ITM (strike > spot for puts). The intrinsic is the difference between strike and spot, not the spot or strike alone. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I Ch4 covers USDINR option pricing. Put intrinsic for currency options works the same as for equity options. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** An option with zero time value near expiry is essentially a synthetic spot position — it will move point-for-point with the underlying.
